paragraphs.body: I knew I had to improve my craft writing of dialogue after beginning to write my first short story. I started multiple drafts where I tried to incorporate dialogue into my story, but it all sounded unnatural to me and unlike the voices of the characters I was trying to write. When reading the “Dialogue” chapter in “Bird by Bird,” I was drawn to Anne Lamott’s quote that “Nothing can break the mood of a piece of writing like bad dialogue,” (pg. 61). This held true in my beginning drafts of my first short story “Snake Island,” so I elected to remove almost all the dialogue, and instead find a way to add dialogue into the next short story I wrote. Not having dialogue worked well for my first short story as it told memories of the main character/narrator in which she most likely wouldn't have remembered exact quotes and phrases. However, for my next short story I wanted to write a story that would accommodate dialogue well.

annotations.comment: A nice opening with a clear thesis. Dialogue is very hard! Don’t beat yourself up about not being immediately great at it.
annotations.context_left: nd instead find a way to add dialogue into the next short story I wrote. Not having dialogue worked
annotations.context_right: for my first short story as it told memories of the main character/narrator in which she most likel

paragraphs.body: In my second short story, “The Boy in the Basement,” I wrote dialogue between kids, so it was important for me to make their dialogue sound believable to be coming from twelve-year-olds. One point that Lamott made in her dialogue chapter that was helpful to me in writing my characters was “...when you’re out in the world… and you hear people talking, you’ll find yourself editing their dialogue,” (pg. 63). I tried to channel the thoughts and words of children I’ve babysat, my younger family members, or even my past self. Writing my character, Maddie, was particularly easy for me as she resembled my middle school self: curious, confident, and slightly bossy. However, writing a character like Carter I found to be more difficult. I had to find a way to make him almost the antithesis of Maddie: uninterested and hesitant. For writing him, I thought of my younger brother, who a few years ago was very similar to the character Carter. Overall, being able to compare my characters to people I knew well and think about the dialogue I wrote in terms of things that actual people would say, was very helpful for me.

annotations.comment: Yes, that’s a great way to do it.
annotations.context_left: In my second short story, “The Boy in the Base

paragraphs.body: When writing dialogue, Lamott also suggests, “Remember that you should be able to identify each character by what he or she says,” (pg. 63). I tried to make this true for my characters Maddie, Everly, and Carter, making them all different from each other and having what they said match their personality. For instance, Maddie being confident and, at times, overconfident had dialogue to match this fact, an example being her overzealousness while trying to save Mr. Bayer’s son who didn’t actually need saving. “‘Stockholm syndrome!’ said Maddie, but she could tell Carter and Everly didn’t know what she was talking about. She whispered to them, ‘He’s grown a bond with his dad who is holding him hostage, so he doesn’t think he needs to be saved.’” Here, Maddie justifies why they need to save Mr. Bayer’s son, despite the boy telling them that he doesn’t need saved. This dialogue shows that she trusts herself and her intuitions, even when she is told otherwise.

annotations.comment: I would introduce this a bit: “As I wrote…” etc. Excellent example.

paragraphs.body: While reading the short story “Halloween” by Marianne Crotty, I tested if Lamott’s  rule of being able to identify the character by what they say held true. Crotty used a good amount of dialogue in her piece, all which matched the characterization of the narrator and her grandmother, Jan. Early in the story, I was able to see that Jan had slightly backwards morals. The narrator’s father, who is Jan’s son, had left his daughter when she was just two. Jan, however, seemed to blame this on the narrator’s mother by saying “That’s fixable though… If your mom wants to put in the work. Men are just like that. They always need a lot of help,” (pg. 97). From these quotes, early in the story I was able to gauge that Jan made excuses for people that she loves. Crotty kept this to be a common theme throughout the story as seen through dialogue between the narrator and Jan and how Jan responds in a similar way to scenarios later in the story as well. The quote at the end of the story “To be with the person you want is heaven. It doesn’t have to be the right circumstances to feel good,” (pg. 107) was very uniquely Jan. Crotty showed throughout her short story Jan’s overly forgiving attitude to people Jan loved even though they had wronged her in the past. Forgiving quotes in the dialogue were identifiable to Jan’s character even without looking to see who said it.

annotations.comment: Yes, great example. (To me, it’s less forgiving and more just morally lax!)
annotations.context_right: short story “Halloween” by Marianne Crotty, I tested if Lamott’s  rule of being able to identify th

paragraphs.body: I also found it helpful when writing dialogue, and especially when proofreading my dialogue in “The Boy in the Basement,” to “read them aloud,” (Lamott, 62). This helped me a lot when revising my short story to make my dialogue sound more natural. Lamott stressed that even though a sentence might look normal to you on the page, “Read aloud, it has no flow, no sense of character’s rhythm that in real life would run through the words,” (pg. 62). Speaking my dialogue out loud helped me to change anything that sounded unnatural for twelve-year-olds to be saying to each other, such as overly sophisticated sentences.

The last piece of advice that Lamott said in her dialogue chapter of Bird by Bird that helped me with the craft of writing dialogue is what I found most helpful in my beginner stages of dialogue writing. “You have to relax, and woolgather, and get rid of the critics, and sit there in some sort of self-hypnosis, and then you have to practice,” (pg. 68). In my short story “The Boy in the Basement,” I wrote countless lines of dialogue that did not make the final draft. Through practice and many poor sounding lines of dialogue, I was able to pull the sentences that stuck. I was able to grow my characters into who I wanted them to be by trying and improving their dialogue until I was satisfied with my characters and the story that I had created.

annotations.comment: Honestly, this is likely THE most realistic writer experience you had — it requires so much thinking and so much revising. I’m really delighted by this paper, Madelyn, and loved having you in class this term. Thank you for your hard work! Have a lovely summer!
